narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 44% chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize risks for the program. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background that correlate with bar success. The most influential element in your case was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 7, which is strong compared to the average of 5.53 and actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass rather than fail. Next in importance was your family's middle-income bracket, which unfortunately pushed the prediction slightly toward failure risk, as the model sees this level—around the dataset's average—as less predictive of bar success than higher brackets in historical patterns. Your Year 1 decile of 7, also above the typical 5.71, provided another positive boost, helping counterbalance some concerns. While your LSAT score of 30.50 falls below the 36.70 average and your full-time student status aligns with most applicants, these played smaller roles here compared to your deciles and family income. Overall, your profile shows consistent mid-to-upper school performance that the model views positively, but the income factor created enough uncertainty to elevate the failure odds above the threshold for approval. You're not far off from typical successful applicants, especially with your GPA strength, but the model prioritizes bar passage signals like these top influencers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that could shift future predictions favorably. Exploring ways to highlight or improve financial stability indicators might also help, perhaps through scholarships or affidavits. Many students in similar positions reapply after targeted prep, turning a 44% risk into a clear pass projection—your solid deciles give you a great foundation for that. Keep pushing; admissions models evolve, and persistence pays off for determined applicants like you.
